title: HBM Memory Sold Out Through 2026 — Pricing Power Returns to DRAM
catalyst: All three HBM producers — SK Hynix, Samsung, and Micron — have confirmed their high-bandwidth memory capacity is sold out through 2026, with shortages expected to extend into 2027. SK Hynix and Samsung now warn the AI-driven memory shortage may persist beyond 2027 as hyperscaler customers reserve supply years ahead. HBM is consuming 23% of total DRAM wafer capacity, pulling wafers away from conventional DRAM and tightening the wider memory market.
thesis: The Microsoft $190B FY2026 capex raise and the broader $700B+ hyperscaler buildout require HBM that physically cannot be produced fast enough. Memory makers have pricing power they haven't had in a decade — Samsung's semiconductor division produced 94% of company profit last quarter and SK Hynix posted record operating income of $27.8B. The spillover into conventional DRAM and NAND (Micron just exited consumer memory entirely) means even non-HBM memory revenue is repricing higher. This is a structural multi-year tightness, not a cyclical pricing blip.

ticker_rationale.MU: Micron is the only US-listed pure-play memory producer and one of just three HBM suppliers globally. It has fully sold out its 2026 HBM capacity, is shipping HBM3E to NVIDIA at high margins, and recently exited the consumer memory market to redirect every wafer toward enterprise and AI customers. With memory pricing in a structural up-cycle and Micron's gross margins expanding, EPS estimates have continued to rise faster than the stock's multiple.

risks: AI capex digestion: if hyperscaler spending pauses or pulls forward demand is satisfied, memory pricing could roll over faster than expected
Samsung's HBM4 yield improvements could break the supply oligopoly and pressure prices in late 2026 / 2027
China's CXMT scaling DDR5 capacity faster than expected could pressure the conventional DRAM tailwind
Memory has historically been cyclical — investors may de-rate the multiple even on rising earnings if they sense a peak
summary: All three HBM producers are sold out through 2026 with shortages extending to 2027+, while Microsoft's $190B capex raise and $700B+ hyperscaler buildout create structural demand that the memory oligopoly cannot satisfy — giving Micron unprecedented pricing power at just 7.9x forward P/E.
body_md:
## Setup

The high-bandwidth memory market has entered an unprecedented state of structural undersupply. All three HBM producers — SK Hynix, Samsung, and Micron — have confirmed their capacity is fully sold out through 2026, with shortages now expected to persist through 2027 and potentially beyond [1]. SK Hynix's available capacity is "nearly zero," and tech giants including Microsoft, Google, and Amazon are reportedly offering to fund new SK Hynix production lines and purchase ASML EUV lithography tools — an extraordinary step that underscores the desperation for supply [3].

The shortage is not confined to HBM. HBM production consumes 23% of total DRAM wafer output in 2026, up from 19% in 2025, and the revenue per wafer for HBM is 3-5x higher than conventional DDR5, making it economically irrational for memory makers to allocate wafers to lower-margin products [4]. This wafer reallocation has starved the conventional DRAM market just as PC and server OEMs are trying to secure supply, tightening an already constrained market and driving DRAM prices up 90% in Q1 2026 versus Q4 2025 [4].

## Why Now

Microsoft's Q3 FY2026 earnings on April 29, 2026 provided the definitive demand signal: CFO Amy Hood guided calendar-year 2026 capex to approximately $190 billion, up 61% from 2025 and roughly $35 billion above consensus [5]. This single company's spending plan exceeds the GDP of most countries and represents a massive draw on HBM and DRAM supply. Across the hyperscaler complex, collective AI infrastructure spending is estimated at $650-700 billion in 2026, up from $360 billion in 2025 [2].

Samsung's semiconductor division posted extraordinary results in Q1 2026: 53.7 trillion won ($36.1 billion) in operating profit, accounting for approximately 94% of the company's total quarterly profit [1]. SK Hynix reported record quarterly revenue of 52.6 trillion won ($35.5 billion) with operating profit of 37.6 trillion won ($27.8 billion) [1]. These results demonstrate that the memory oligopoly has achieved pricing power not seen in over a decade — and the supply deficit is widening, not narrowing.

Micron's strategic decision to exit the consumer memory market entirely and redirect every available wafer toward enterprise and AI customers is perhaps the clearest signal that this cycle is structurally different from previous memory booms [2]. When the third-largest producer voluntarily abandons a market segment to focus on higher-margin AI applications, it signals that the capacity constraint is real and the prioritization is permanent for the foreseeable future.

## Company-Specific Edge

Micron Technology (MU) occupies a unique position as the only US-listed pure-play memory producer and one of just three global HBM suppliers. Q2 FY2026 was record-breaking across every metric: $23.9 billion in revenue (+196% YoY), $13.8 billion in net income ($12.07 EPS), and gross margins of 74.9% — with Q3 guidance calling for $33.5 billion in revenue and approximately 81% gross margins [2]. If achieved, Q3 would represent the most profitable quarter in semiconductor industry history.

Micron's HBM4 is ramping into NVIDIA Vera Rubin shipments through 2026, with HBM4e targeting 2027. The company has fully sold out its 2026 HBM capacity, and operating cash flow of $11.9 billion in Q2 alone provides substantial capital for capacity expansion without dilution. The balance sheet is clean with just 14.9% debt-to-equity, and the IOWN score of 82 (BUY) reflects strong underlying business quality across innovation, infrastructure, and competitive positioning.

The most striking aspect of Micron's current valuation is the disconnect between fundamentals and multiple. At 7.9x forward P/E with 81% guided gross margins and multi-year contracted revenue, the stock is still priced using an old commodity-cycle framework that assumes margin mean-reversion. The structural arguments against mean-reversion — oligopoly supply, sold-out capacity through 2026-2027, and hyperscaler customers willing to fund new fabs — have not been fully internalized by the market.

## Numbers That Matter

**Micron Technology (MU)**:
- Stock price: $803.63
- Forward P/E: 7.9x
- P/E ratio: 37.87x (trailing, reflecting prior-year lower earnings)
- Q2 FY2026 revenue: $23.9B (+196% YoY)
- Q3 FY2026 guidance: $33.5B revenue, ~81% gross margin, $19.15 EPS
- Q2 net income: $13.8B ($12.07 EPS)
- Operating cash flow Q2: $11.9B
- Profit margin: 41.49%
- Return on equity: 39.82%
- Debt-to-equity: 14.9%
- IOWN overall score: 82 (BUY)

**Market context**:
- HBM consuming 23% of DRAM wafers (up from 19%)
- DRAM prices: +90% QoQ in Q1 2026, +58-63% QoQ in Q2 2026
- Samsung semi operating profit: $36.1B (94% of total company)
- SK Hynix operating income: $27.8B (record)
- Microsoft 2026 capex: $190B (+61% YoY)

## Bear Case

Memory has historically been the most violently cyclical sector in semiconductors. Even within confirmed supercycles, inventory corrections producing 30-50% equity drawdowns have occurred repeatedly, and the stocks typically peak 3-6 months before the pricing cycle turns. Micron's stock has already risen 247% over the past year, embedding substantial optimism [2].

Samsung's HBM4 yield improvements are progressing, and new capacity from both Samsung and SK Hynix coming online through 2027 could break the current supply oligopoly and pressure prices. If hyperscaler spending merely flattens — not declines, but stops accelerating — the marginal memory supplier (Micron, with the smallest HBM market share) would feel the impact first. Harvard semiconductor expert Dennis Yao has publicly warned that "this too will pass," reminding investors that structural narratives in memory have been wrong before [2].

China's CXMT is scaling DDR5 capacity faster than anticipated, which could ease conventional DRAM pricing even if HBM remains tight. AI training architectures are also evolving toward memory-efficient designs — mixture-of-experts, sparse models, and quantization — that could reduce HBM intensity per unit of compute over 12-24 months. Finally, memory de-rates have historically been binary: when the market senses a peak, multiples compress violently even on rising near-term earnings.

## Conclusion

The HBM memory shortage thesis is the highest-conviction opportunity in our screen. The supply mechanism is verifiable (HBM consumes 23% of wafers at 3-5x revenue per wafer), the demand driver is confirmed ($190B Microsoft capex, $700B hyperscaler total), and the supply response timeline is structurally long (4-5 years to build new fabs). Micron at 7.9x forward P/E with 81% guided gross margins is the most mispriced large-cap semiconductor name in the market. The key monitoring points are Samsung's HBM4 yield announcements, quarterly hyperscaler capex guidance, and any signs of memory hoarding unwinding in channel inventory checks.
invalidation: Micron's quarterly gross margins fall below 60% for two consecutive quarters, signaling the pricing cycle has turned
SK Hynix or Samsung announces HBM capacity is no longer sold out or begins accepting spot orders without long-term contracts, indicating demand normalization
Aggregate hyperscaler AI capex guidance is reduced by more than 20% in a single quarter, undermining the structural demand thesis

sources.title: Samsung and SK Hynix warn AI-driven memory shortages could last until 2027 and beyond
AI memory is sold out, causing an unprecedented surge in prices
SK Hynix Capacity Hits Zero: Tech Giants Offer to Fund Factories Amid AI Chip Shortage
Memory Chip Shortage 2026: HBM Takes 23% of DRAM Wafers
